purpose: V5-FOUND-2 Phase 0 baseline. Captures `cargo test --workspace --all-targets` wall-time on a representative dev laptop BEFORE nextest adoption. V5-FOUND-2 Phase 5 (CI integration) and V5-FOUND-3 (per-test coverage selection) compare against this — primary question: did nextest's parallel scheduling actually shorten the wall-time, or did it just rearrange the same work?

captured_against.cpu: Intel Core i5-8300H @ 2.30GHz (4 cores, 8 threads, H-class mobile)
captured_against.memory_label: 8 GB total visible (representative dev workstation; tighter than the V5-FOUND-1 baseline host but same family)
captured_against.git_sha_baseline_short_note: V5-SDK-1 Phase 5 close-out; V5-FOUND-2 Phase 0 starts here (no production code touched between this commit and baseline capture).
captured_against.build_profile: debug (cargo's default for `cargo test`; unoptimized + debuginfo)
captured_against.test_runner: cargo test (libtest harness — the THING WE'RE REPLACING with cargo-nextest in V5-FOUND-2 Phase 1)

capture_protocol.warmup_treatment: Run 1 captures the post-V5-SDK-1-work state; some crates may have stale artifacts. Runs 2-3 are warm (no source changes, all crates cached). This pattern matches the actual dev-loop reality more than wiped-target/cold runs would.
capture_protocol.concurrent_load: no other Rust builds active during capture; no IDE running

what_run_1_includes_that_warm_doesn_t: After V5-SDK-1 work concluded with `cargo test -p neurogrim-sdk`, only the SDK + its 3 deps (core, a2a, secrets) were freshly compiled. `cargo test --workspace --all-targets` on Run 1 had to compile the OTHER workspace members (neurogrim-cli, neurogrim-dashboard, neurogrim-ecosystem, neurogrim-mcp, neurogrim-sensory) plus all their test binaries from stale rmeta. Run 1's 151s is the realistic 'first run after a single-crate edit' time. Runs 2-3's 92-98s is the 'no-source-changes re-run' time.
test_count_estimate: ~1,470 tests workspace-wide (citation: V5-MOD-3 close commit notes). Exact count not extracted from --quiet output; can be re-derived via `cargo test --workspace --all-targets 2>&1 | grep -E '^test result:' | wc -l` if needed.
v5_found_2_post_nextest_comparison_quest: Phase 5 question: does `cargo nextest run --workspace --all-targets --profile ci` warm wall-time beat 95s p50 on this same host?
Phase 5 question: does the post-nextest CI rust: job (currently runs cargo test) wall-time on GitHub Actions improve commensurately?
Phase 4 question: which individual tests exceed 5s investigate / 10s violate threshold (Fork D1)?
what_this_baseline_does_NOT_capture: Cold-from-clean-target/ wall-time (would require `cargo clean` + full rebuild; recapture if needed)
Per-test wall-time distribution (Phase 4 audit captures this with `--status-level slow`)
CI wall-time on GitHub Actions runners (different hardware; Phase 5 captures separately)
wall-time under test-failure conditions (only happy-path measured)
recapture_required_if_changes_to: Major workspace member additions (e.g., a new crate joining the workspace shifts the baseline)
Dev profile in Cargo.toml (e.g., adding `[profile.dev] opt-level = 1` would change build characteristics)
Test count substantially (e.g., +20% test additions invalidates direct comparison)

cross_references.predecessor_baseline: roadmap/data/v5-scoring-baseline-2026-05-02.json (V5-MOD-1 perf-gate; different metric — scoring-path latency, not test-suite wall-time)
